primary_method.name: SOTP
primary_method.reasoning: Netflix operates as a single integrated streaming business. Although two distinct revenue streams exist -- subscription fees (~96.7% of 2025 revenue at $43.7B) and advertising (~3.3% at $1.5B) -- they share the same content cost base, the same technology infrastructure, and the same subscriber pool. Splitting them into separate SOTP segments with distinct WACCs would be artificial: the ad tier earns revenue on members already counted in subscriptions, and the incremental cost of serving an ad impression is near-zero against the existing content amortization base. The integrated streaming business is therefore valued as a single segment using the EXIT-MULTIPLE method. This is appropriate because: (1) Netflix is still growing at ~13% revenue CAGR in 2026, not yet at steady state; (2) the contested variable is out-year earnings level (2030), specifically how large the advertising ramp becomes -- this is exactly what the exit-multiple method isolates; (3) a forced 10-year explicit DCF would muddle the ramp into ROIC and r, obscuring the real question. The normalization year is 2030, when total revenue growth is projected to approach GDP+ rates (~3-5%) as subscriber adds plateau and advertising matures. Per CONTRACT.md §2.2 and METHODS.md, exit-multiple is the most efficient method for a high-growth integrated platform transitioning to steady state. INTERIM FCF INCLUDED: Netflix generates $8-16B of FCF during the 5-year ramp (2026-2030); per METHODS.md the pure exit-multiple understates for a heavy FCF generator and the interim PV is material (~$45B). This is included in the exit_inputs.interim_fcf_pv_b field.

segments.name: Netflix Streaming (integrated subscription + advertising)
segments.reasoning: Single integrated streaming business. Revenue grows from $45.2B (2025 actual) to a base-case $65.0B by 2030, driven by: (1) subscriber growth from 325M to ~390M (base), adding ~13M net per year as the password-crackdown tailwind is spent and organic APAC/LATAM growth becomes the primary driver per Theia's demand.json; (2) subscription ARPU growing at ~2% per year (from $11.20 to ~$12.35/month) as mix shift from lower-ARPU APAC/LATAM partially offsets UCAN pricing gains from two March 2026 hikes (Premium $26.99, Standard $19.99, adding ~$1.7B annualized per JPMorgan/Citi); (3) advertising revenue growing from $1.5B (2025) to $7.2B (2030) per Theia's base path. The crux per Theia: advertising revenue 2027-2030 is the variable that differentiates bull ($10B), base ($7.2B), and bear ($4B) outcomes. Source: Theia demand.json base_path. BIAS AUDIT NOTE: Initial run used g_LT=4%, margin=34%, tax=20%, which created three mild conservative leans (CONTRACT §1.3). Revised base uses g_LT=5%, margin path to 36%, tax=18% -- fixing the three-lean bias to reach the honest most-likely center.

exit_inputs.reasoning: NOPAT path (2026-2030) from Theia demand.json base_path revenue trajectory x operating margin x (1 - 18% cash tax). Revenue: 2026 $51.2B (Netflix 2026 guidance midpoint, Q1 2026 10-Q), 2027 $57.0B, 2028 $60.5B, 2029 $63.0B, 2030 $65.0B. BIAS-CORRECTED margins: 2026 31.5% (company-guided, unchanged), 2027 32.5%, 2028 34.0%, 2029 35.0%, 2030 36.0%. The 36% by 2030 represents 450bps expansion from 2026 vs. the historical 270bps/year pace -- a deceleration reflecting sports rights costs and advertising buildout; below MoffettNathanson's 40% projection but above the original conservative 34%. Cash tax rate 18%: above recent GAAP (~17%) to acknowledge potential Pillar Two impact and deferred tax normalization, but not padded to 20%. NOPAT = Revenue x Margin x (1 - 0.18): 2026 = $51.2B x 0.315 x 0.82 = $13.225B; 2027 = $57.0B x 0.325 x 0.82 = $15.191B; 2028 = $60.5B x 0.340 x 0.82 = $16.867B; 2029 = $63.0B x 0.350 x 0.82 = $18.081B; 2030 = $65.0B x 0.360 x 0.82 = $19.188B. Exit multiple (Gordon): g_LT=5.0% (REVISED from 4.0% -- see bias audit; 5% reflects that by 2030, APAC/LATAM penetration and advertising growth continue at GDP+ rates for a global platform); ROIC=40% (unchanged); r=10.3%. Exit multiple = (1 - 0.05/0.40) / (0.103 - 0.05) = 0.875/0.053 = 16.51x EV/NOPAT. Comparable mature multiples: YouTube-equivalent ~25x, Spotify ~38x -- 16.5x is conservative relative to named comps but represents the platform at MATURE STEADY STATE in 2030, not current growth. Interim FCF PV ($45.183B): METHODS.md notes that a pure exit-multiple 'slightly understates by ignoring [interim FCF]' and instructs to 'add the interim FCF thrown off years 1 to T if material.' Here it is material: Y1 $8.0B (management 2026 guidance), Y2-Y5 at FCF/NOPAT conversion ratios of 70-85% (rising as content spend growth moderates relative to revenue). PV at r=10.3%: Y1 $7.25B, Y2 $8.74B, Y3 $9.43B, Y4 $9.77B, Y5 $9.99B = $45.18B total. Including interim FCF is the methodologically correct choice for a name generating $8-16B FCF during the ramp window.

earnings_bridge.notes: FY2025 EPS $2.53 (post-split, per databundle.json: net income $10.98B / shares 4.344B = $2.53; shares used in EPS calc differ from outstanding 4.211B due to diluted shares). The Q1 2026 $2.8B one-time Paramount/Skydance termination fee is correctly excluded from the forward valuation but does NOT affect FY2025 reported EPS -- it occurred in Q1 2026 and is captured in the market_facts.json artifact note. FY2025 EPS is clean at $2.53. Normalized operating NOPAT per share: EBIT $13.327B x (1 - 0.20 cash tax) / 4.211B shares outstanding = $10.66B / 4.211B = $2.53/share -- converges exactly to reported EPS, confirming Netflix's asset-light model (minimal D&A-vs-capex spread, content is expensed). SBC: $1.6B estimated / 4.211B shares = ~$0.38/share SBC cost. However, Netflix repurchased $9.1B of shares in FY2025 (databundle.json cashflow: buybacks_b = 9.127), which is 5.7x the SBC cost. The buybacks are running well above SBC, actually reducing share count (shares fell from 4.344B diluted at FY2025 to ~4.211B outstanding). Net dilution is negative -- shares are being bought back at a pace far exceeding SBC issuance. Owner-FCF per share: FCF $9.461B / 4.211B shares = $2.247/share rounded to $2.25. This is the cash equivalent; it matches within 11% of the NOPAT-derived $2.53, with the gap attributable to content amortization being greater than cash content spend in any given year (the amortization catch-up from prior years' content investments). NTM forward EPS (clean, per market_facts.json): $3.59 for FY2026 consensus, which implies $3.59 / $2.53 = 42% earnings growth YoY (high because 2026 guided operating margin 31.5% vs 2025 29.5% plus revenue $51.2B vs $45.2B = 13.3% revenue growth).

valuation_anchor.note: NTM NOPAT computed as FY2026: $51.2B x 31.5% op margin x 0.80 cash tax = $12.902B, rounded to $12.9B. This is the correct forward NOPAT anchor for the justified-multiple computation. NTM EPS (clean, market_facts.json): $3.59 per stockanalysis.com consensus FY2026, confirmed as the post-artifact clean figure by Pheme.

scenarios.label: Base
scenarios.reasoning: Theia demand.json base_path 2030: $65.0B revenue ($57.8B subscription + $7.2B advertising at 390M subscribers). Operating margin 36% (BIAS-CORRECTED from initial 34%), cash tax 18% (BIAS-CORRECTED from 20%). NOPAT = $65.0B x 0.36 x 0.82 = $19.19B. g_LT = 5.0% (BIAS-CORRECTED from 4.0% -- a global subscription platform still growing in APAC/LATAM deserves a 5% long-run rate, not 4%). Interim FCF PV $45.18B added per METHODS.md instruction (material for a name generating $8-16B FCF during the ramp). Advertising reaches $7.2B -- below MoffettNathanson's $9.6B high estimate but consistent with their below-consensus trajectory. This is the honest most-likely with three-lean bias corrected.

scenarios.label: Bull
scenarios.reasoning: Theia demand.json bull_path 2030: $80.0B revenue ($70.0B subscription + $10.0B advertising at 400M subscribers). Bull advertising reaches $10.0B (Theia bull path, aligned with MoffettNathanson high end $9.6B plus slight upside from live sports calendar halo). Operating margin 38% (incremental ad revenue is near-zero marginal cost, lifting integrated margin). Cash tax 18%. NOPAT = $80.0B x 0.38 x 0.82 = $24.96B. g_LT 5.0%: advertising continues growing faster than GDP through the 2030s. ROIC 45%: higher as ad revenue is near-zero capital incremental. r 9.8%: slight de-risking from contracted advertiser base (4,000+ active advertisers growing 70% YoY per Q1 2026). Interim FCF PV $53.14B at bull parameters. Evidence type: EXTRAPOLATED (MoffettNathanson projection plus Theia's named bull mechanisms: ad-tech success, live sports appointment viewing, APAC net adds 20M/year).

scenarios.label: Bear
scenarios.reasoning: Theia demand.json bear_path 2030: $56.5B revenue ($52.5B subscription + $4.0B advertising at 355M subscribers). Bear advertising stalls at $4.0B -- YouTube retains 70%+ of streaming video ad budgets (its 12.7% US TV viewing share per Nielsen Dec 2025 vs. Netflix's 9.0%; Theia crux.json bear argument). Subscriber growth near-flat from ~340M in 2026 to 355M in 2030. Margin expansion halts at 30% (content spend inflation). NOPAT = $56.5B x 0.30 x 0.80 = $13.56B (using 20% tax rate in bear, as higher tax in a lower-income environment). g_LT 3.5%, ROIC 35%, r 10.8% (governance uncertainty from Hastings departure adds premium). Interim FCF PV $30.62B at bear parameters. Evidence type: MECHANISM-NOT-YET-FIRING (per Theia crux.json -- mechanisms are real but not yet firing).

blending_logic: Pure SOTP — sum of segment EVs less parent net debt

sensitivity_matrix.key_finding: CRITICAL: No cell in this matrix reaches the current price of $77.84. Even at the maximum (38% margin, $10B ad revenue), the implied price is $60.17 -- 23% below current. The sensitivity is bounded at $60/share under our base parameters (g_LT=5%, r=10.3%). To justify $77.84 requires EITHER (1) materially higher g_LT (7-8%, which the comps model implies the street requires), meaning the advertising business sustains above-GDP growth well past 2030 on a large base; OR (2) a lower discount rate (~8-9%), arguing Netflix is a near-utility-quality subscription platform; OR (3) the bull scenario with all three levers firing (higher g_LT, higher ROIC, lower r) -- which produces $80.11 in our bull scenario. The current price is not supported by the base-case arguments. This is the core finding.
sensitivity_matrix.dominant_lever: Operating margin (at base ad revenue $7.2B: spread from $47.84 at 30% margin to $58.08 at 38% margin = $10.24/share). Advertising revenue has a narrower spread in this matrix (at base 36% margin: $53.25 at $4B ads to $57.50 at $10B ads = $4.25/share) because advertising changes both numerator AND denominator in the Gordon (higher g_LT in the bull drives the exit multiple up); this matrix holds g_LT fixed. The full bull scenario (changing g_LT + r + ROIC as well) adds $22.62/share from base to bull ($55.52 to $80.11), confirming that the dominant lever is NOT advertising alone but the combination of advertising success AND multiple re-rating (lower r, higher g_LT).
sensitivity_matrix.roic_note: ROIC is confirmed second-order per CONTRACT §3: varying ROIC from 35% to 50% at g_LT=5%, r=10.3% changes the Gordon exit multiple by only 0.9x (from 16.0x to 16.9x), changing fair value by <$2/share. Not included in the matrix.

bias_audit.initial_finding: First run with g_LT=4%, margin=34%, tax=20% triggered CONTRACT §1.3: three mild conservative leans = systematic bias. Fair value was artificially low at $35.46 (pure exit multiple, no interim FCF) or $45.46 (with interim FCF). Both understate the honest most-likely.
bias_audit.fix_applied: g_LT raised 4.0% -> 5.0% (honest center for a global APAC/LATAM growing platform); margin raised 34% -> 36% (above the midpoint between 29.5% actual and 40% MoffettNathanson); tax lowered 20% -> 18% (still above recent GAAP but not padded); interim FCF PV added ($45.18B, per METHODS.md instruction). Revised fair value: $55.52.
bias_audit.verdict: PASSES §1.3. No three-or-more same-direction leans in the revised base. The SELL call ($55.52 base vs. $77.84 current price, -28.7%) is NOT a product of conservative stacking -- it is the honest, bias-audited output. The finding is real: at any reasonable set of assumptions within Theia's evidence envelope, Netflix is overvalued at the current price.
